Job's friends now allowed him to speak, and he proceeded in a serious and helpful manner. Job had confidence in the goodness of both his case and of his God; and cheerfully committed his case to him. But Job did not show due reverence when he spoke of God as taking away his judgment and troubling his soul. To ensure that our hearts do not condemn us while we maintain our integrity, frustrates the plans of the evil spirit.

Job considered the condition of a hypocrite and a wicked person to be very miserable. If they prospered in life through their profession and maintained a presumptuous hope until death, what would that profit when God demanded their souls? The more comfort we find in our religion, the more closely we will hold to it. Those who have no delight in God are easily led astray by pleasures and easily overcome by the difficulties of this life.

Job's friends, on the same subject, spoke of the misery of wicked people before death as being equal to their crimes; Job considered that even if it were not so, still the consequences of their death would be terrible. Job undertook to present this matter in a true light. Death to a godly person is like a favorable wind carrying him to heaven; but to a wicked person, it is like a storm that rushes him away to destruction. While he lived, he had the benefit of mercy; but now God’s patience is over, and he will pour out his wrath upon him. When God casts down a person, there is no escaping or enduring his anger. Those who will not now flee to the arms of Divine grace, which are stretched out to receive them, will not be able to flee from the arms of Divine wrath, which will shortly be stretched out to destroy them. And what does a person gain if he acquires the whole world and loses his own soul?
